commentaries.author: Ephrem The Syrian
commentaries.content: 1 Esdras Wisdom of Solomon Wisdom of Sirach Epistle of Jeremiah Prayer of Manasseh And when he saw that, he arose, and went for his life, and came to Beer-sheba, which belongs to Judah, and left his servant there. “Then he was afraid; he got up and fled for his life. ” It was out of wisdom that he turned away from danger, because it was absolutely wrong that he disposed of his soul, since he had no reason to give it to death, but, on the contrary, he had every reason to keep it alive. And that was done so that the prophets of falsehood might not say that the god—whose worship he had disrupted, whose sacrifices he had despised and whose prophets he had killed—had handed him over to the power of the queen.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content:
Afraid. Hebrew, "he saw, arose, and went for his life. "(Haydock)
He was aware of a woman's anger, Ecclesiasticus xxv. 23. Though he goes intrepidly to meet Achab, he flees before a woman, God being desirous that he should exercise humility, (Theodoret, q. 57) though some think that he had given way to a secret fault; (Calmet) which is a groundless assertion. (Haydock)
He must confess that all his strength is from above. (Tirinus)
Mind, to escape notice. (Menochius)
Bersabee, at the southern extremity of the kingdom of Juda, perhaps fifty leagues from Samaria, and five more from Jezrahel. (Calmet)
Servant, the boy whom he had raised to life. (Abulensis)

methodology.step_1: Parse main page with BeautifulSoup for structure
methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
